claim: The material was electroslag remelted, homogenized at 1200 °C for 1 h, hot-rolled in 5 passes at 1100–1200 °C followed by air quenching, and annealed at 950 °C for 1 h to obtain a triplex microstructure of ferrite, austenite, and martensite.
evidence_text: ESRed material was homogenized at 1200 ◦C for 1 h due to the elimination of any probable segregation in the microstructure, then hot-rolled through 5 passes in the temperature range of 1100–1200 ◦C followed by air quenching, and finally annealed at 950°C for 1 h for attaining a triplex (ferrite + austenite + martensite) microstructure.

claim: Warm compression tests were performed at temperatures from 100 to 700 °C, at a strain rate of 0.001 s⁻¹, to a true strain of 0.6, with specimens soaked for 5 min at the deformation temperature and then water-quenched.
evidence_text: compression tests were carried out by ... in a wide range of temperature (100–700 °C) under the strain rate of 0.001 s−1 up to the true strain 0.6. The specimens were then immediately water quenched ... soaked at deformation temperature for 5 min

claim: Microstructures were characterized using field-emission SEM with EBSD at a step size of 200 nm and TEM (JEM-2100-F) operated at 200 kV.
evidence_text: Microstructures were investigated with the aim of (i) field-emission scanning electron microscope (FE-SEM CZ/MIRA I LMH, TESCAN, Czech) equipped with an electron backscattered diffraction (EBSD) detector ... using a step size of 200 nm and (ii) transmission electron microscopy (TEM: JEM-2100-F with acceleration voltage: 200 KV equipped field-emission gun

claim: TEM thin foils were prepared by twin-jet polishing using a solution of 10% HClO4 and 90% methyl alcohol at −30 °C and an applied potential of 16 V.
evidence_text: Thin-foils for TEM analysis were prepared by twin-jet polishing using a solution of HClO4 (10%) and methyl alcohol (90%) at −30 °C and an applied potential of 16V.

claim: The Fe-11.15Mn-5.6Al-0.07C low-density steel was electroslag remelted, homogenized at 1200 °C for 1 h, hot-rolled in 5 passes at 1100–1200 °C followed by air quenching, and annealed at 950 °C for 1 h to achieve a triplex ferrite, austenite, and martensite microstructure.
evidence_text: Fe-11.15Mn-5.6Al-0.07C (wt.%) low density steel was received in an electroslag remelted (ESR) condition. ESRed material was homogenized at 1200 °C for 1 h due to the elimination of any probable segregation in the microstructure, then hot-rolled through 5 passes in the temperature range of 1100–1200 °C followed by air quenching, and finally annealed at 950 °C for 1 h for attaining a triplex (ferrite + austenite + martensite) microstructure.

claim: Hot compression tests were performed over 100–700 °C at a strain rate of 0.001 s⁻¹ to a true strain of 0.6, with a 5 min soak at deformation temperature and immediate water quenching to preserve the deformed microstructure.
evidence_text: The high temperature compression tests were carried out by Gotech AI-7000 universal testing machine equipped with a programmable furnace in a wide range of temperature (100–700 °C) under the strain rate of 0.001 s−1 up to the true strain 0.6. The specimens were then immediately water quenched to preserve of deformed microstructure at high temperatures. The compression specimens were soaked at deformation temperature for 5 min before testing to reach the thermal equilibrium condition.

claim: Microstructural characterization was performed with FE-SEM/EBSD using a 200 nm step size and with TEM at 200 kV; TEM thin foils were prepared by twin-jet electropolishing with 10% HClO4 in methanol at −30 °C and 16 V.
evidence_text: Microstructures were investigated with the aim of (i) field-emission scanning electron microscope... using a step size of 200 nm and (ii) transmission electron microscopy (TEM: JEM-2100-F with acceleration voltage: 200 KV...). Thin-foils for TEM analysis were prepared by twin-jet polishing using a solution of HClO4 (10%) and methyl alcohol (90%) at −30 °C and an applied potential of 16V.

claim: The starting material exhibited a triplex ferrite–austenite–thermally induced martensite microstructure with extremely low dislocation density, and the BCC martensite was differentiated from ferrite using band slope phase contrast and SAED.
evidence_text: The triplex starting microstructure of the experimented material consists of ferrite, austenite and thermally induced martensite is given in Fig. 1a–b. The diffraction patterns... verify the existence of martensite... The band slope phase contrast as a qualitative approach has been employed to differentiate between the martensite and ferrite phases... The initial fresh microstructure is characterized by the extremely low dislocation density of ferrite and austenite phases.

claim: The true stress-strain curves show mechanical stability between 200–500 °C and a significant drop in flow stress when the temperature increases from 500 to 600 °C; appreciable flow softening occurs after a true strain of 0.3 in specimens compressed at 500–600 °C.
evidence_text: The mechanical stability over the temperature range of 200–500 °C is the main characteristic of the flow curve... a significant drop in stress level is recognized with increasing the temperature from 500 to 600 °C... An appreciable flow softening after the true strain of 0.3 is recognized in compressive flow curves which have been obtained at 500–600 °C.

claim: Thermomechanical processing at 500 °C produced frequently distributed deformation bands throughout the microstructure whereas at 600 °C such bands were absent due to higher strain distribution capability at elevated temperature.
evidence_text: thermomechanical processing at the lower temperature of 500 ◦C has resulted in the frequently distributed deformation bands through the microstructure (Fig. 2a), the feature that has not been observed at 600 ◦C (Fig. 2b) due to the higher capability for strain distribution at higher temperature.

claim: At 500 °C and a true strain of 0.3, dislocations in ferrite form tangled cell structures; multiple slip systems {110}<111> and {112}<111> are activated, producing intersecting slip bands and high dislocation density walls (HDDWs).
evidence_text: at the interrupted strain of 0.3, many dislocations were tangled to form the cell structures within the ferrite (Fig. 3a), and a planar slip-like character has started to form. The strain within the ferrite is mainly accommodated through activation of multiple slip systems: {110} <111> and {112} <111>, therefore, formation and intersection of slip bands is highly probable (follow the single-slip traces in Fig. 3a). ... high dislocation density walls (HDDWs) can be traced through the microstructure.

claim: After compression at 500 °C to ε=0.6, a banded structure is formed; parent austenite is sheared and simultaneous deformation-induced ferrite-to-austenite (red arrows) and austenite-to-ferrite (green arrows) transformations occur along deformation bands.
evidence_text: The higher magnification of the microstructures which have been compressed down at 500°C to true strain of 0.6 are brought in Fig. 3c and d, where the banded structure is clearly revealed by dashed-black ovals. As it can be realized, the parent austenite has been effectively sheared through the propagation of the deformation bands. Interestingly, the simultaneous occurrence of the deformation induced “ferrite to austenite” and “austenite to ferrite” transformations are recognized along with the deformation bands which are depicted by red and green arrows, respectively.

claim: Thermo-Calc predicts a BCC_A1#2 phase with an extremely high Mn content in addition to BCC_A2 ferrite and FCC_A1 austenite.
evidence_text: Different phases are predicted by Thermo-Calc in both diagrams: (1) BCC_A2 and (2) FCC_A1... Furthermore, (3) BCC_A1#2 is a phase with the BCC structure which owns an extremely high amount of Mn.

claim: Bi-directional ferrite to austenite and austenite to ferrite deformation induced transformations were observed in Fe-11.15Mn-5.6Al-0.07C low density steel after thermomechanical processing in the temperature range of 100–700 °C.
evidence_text: The bi-directional “ferrite to austenite” and “austenite to ferrite” deformation induced transformations were analyzed in Fe-11.15Mn-5.6Al-0.07C (wt.%) low density steel employing detailed microstructural analysis and through a proper thermodynamic Gibbs energy model. In this respect, the thermomechanical processing was conducted in a wide temperature range of 100– 700 °C.

claim: Thermo-Calc results for the Fe-11.15Mn-5.6Al-0.07C system show the mass percentages of Mn and Al in each constituent phase in equilibrium, and at isothermal sections of 500 and 550 °C the Gibbs Free Energy of FCC and BCC varies with Mn and Al content, with point A indicating thermodynamic equality of Gibbs energies.
evidence_text: Fig. 4. The Thermo-Calc® results show the mass percentages of (a) Mn and (b) Al in each constituent phases in the equilibrium condition for experimented Fe-11.15Mn-5.6Al-0.07C system. The variation of Gibbs Free Energy of FCC and BCC at isothermal section of 500 and 550 °C with various content of (c, e) Mn% and (d, f) Al%. A: shows the point that Gibbs Free Energy of FCC and BCC is equal thermodynamically.

claim: After deformation at 500 °C, 0.001 s⁻¹ to true strain 0.6, SEM analysis showed Mn content of 10.3±0.1 wt.% in ferrite and 12.8±0.1 wt.% in austenite, and Al content of 6.0±0.1 wt.% in ferrite and 4.7±0.1 wt.% in austenite.
evidence_text: Table 1 Chemical composition of experimented steel after deformation at 500 °C, –0.001 s−1 up to the true strain of 0.6 from the SEM analysis.
